question: Explain the primary difference between a Stack and a Queue, and provide a real-world application example for each data structure.
expectedPoints: A Stack is a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) structure where the last element added is the first one removed.
A Queue is a FIFO (First-In, First-Out) structure where the first element added is the first one removed.

Real-world application: A Stack is used for undo functionality in text editors (popping actions to reverse them). A Queue is used in print job scheduling (jobs are processed in the order they are received).

question: Compare and contrast the time complexities for insertion, deletion, and search operations in a Binary Search Tree (BST) and a Hash Table. Discuss one scenario where a BST would be a more suitable choice than a Hash Table.
expectedPoints: BST: Insertion, deletion, and search have an average time complexity of O(log n) and a worst-case of O(n) if the tree becomes unbalanced.
Hash Table: Insertion, deletion, and search have an average time complexity of O(1), but worst-case can be O(n) due to collisions.
A BST is more suitable when you need to maintain sorted data for operations like in-order traversal, range queries, or finding the closest value, which are inefficient in a standard hash table.

question: Describe the step-by-step process of performing a deletion of a node with two children in a Binary Search Tree. Explain why this operation is more complex than deleting a leaf node and how the tree's ordering properties are maintained.
expectedPoints: The process involves finding the node to delete, then finding its in-order successor (the smallest node in its right subtree) or in-order predecessor (the largest node in its left subtree).
The value of the successor/predecessor is copied into the node targeted for deletion.
The original successor/predecessor node (which is now a duplicate value) is then deleted. This node will have at most one child, simplifying its deletion.

It is more complex than deleting a leaf node (which just requires setting a parent's pointer to null) because it requires finding a replacement value and handling a subsequent deletion to preserve the BST property.

The ordering is maintained because the in-order successor is the next largest value in the tree, so replacing the deleted node's value with it ensures all values in the left subtree are smaller and all values in the right subtree are larger.
